data.title: Tax-Loss Harvesting With Bonds Vs. Stocks: Different Rules, Same Goal
data.description:
By Paul Robertson, Gavin Romm, CFA and Mark Gleason, CFA

Investors shouldn’t overlook bonds as a key part of the tax management arsenal.

Tax management is about more than just deferring taxes to reduce this year’s bite. It’s also about managing where and how taxes show up over time. For high-net-worth investors with diversified portfolios, permanently reducing taxes versus deferring them may bolster long-term after-tax wealth. Many investors overlook a potent tax reduction tool: bonds.

The Mechanics of Tax-Loss Harvesting with Stocks

With tax-loss harvesting, realizing a loss on one stock can offset a realized gain elsewhere, reducing the current year’s capital gains expense.

Let’s say you previously bought 10 shares of ABC Corporation’s stock at $100 per share. Its cost basis is $1,000 (10 shares times $100). You sell the stock for $50 per share and reinvest the proceeds in a replacement stock; its cost basis would be $500 (the reinvested amount).

Your portfolio’s cost basis drops by $500, but its market value has already fallen. This increases the net unrealized gain, so future turnover will likely create a bigger realized gain or a smaller realized loss. You didn’t eliminate taxes, but you did alter when and how you’ll pay them.

Taxes Deferred Keep More After-Tax Dollars at Work

But the “kicking the can down the road” strategy of tax deferral can be effective, even if that can is bigger later on. With a reduced tax burden this year, you keep more after-tax dollars at work in your portfolio. If you invest smartly, you have the potential to boost your after-tax wealth by more than enough to pay the bigger bill later.

In truth, there’s really only one way to avoid having to pay more taxes in later years. That’s to never sell the replacement stock. If you die before selling it, the portfolio qualifies for a step-up in cost basis, eliminating the unrealized gain. But that “fix,” as we see it, restricts the ability of your investments to build the wealth you need to support your lifestyle.

Bonds’ Design Creates a Low Profile for Capital Gains and Losses

This dynamic raises a key question: is there a way to harvest losses without boosting future tax exposure? In our view, bonds can play a powerful and often overlooked role in this respect, making them a key part of the tax-loss harvesting tool kit in their own right (Display).

Much of it has to do with the way bonds work. When you sell a bond at a loss and reinvest the proceeds in another bond, that investment will mature at its face value, or par. Often, it does so without producing a taxable gain. The key is to buy the replacement bond generally at a price that’s above its par value and higher than the de minimis threshold.

Using this technique means that the difference between your portfolio’s market value and cost basis is not a reliable guide to your future capital gains. Of course, any bond can be sold before it matures at a price that creates a realized gain or loss. But bonds can also be held to maturity and—if they were bought above the de minimis threshold—don’t result in a capital gain or loss.

Bonds, particularly municipals, typically generate fewer capital gains over time, so investors can harvest losses today without significantly increasing future tax exposure. As a result, they may be especially effective for reducing or even fully offsetting smaller capital gains in a given year.

The Big Picture

As we’ve said before, investors should leave no stone unturned in boosting after-tax returns. In that effort, both stocks and bonds are useful in tax-loss harvesting. If you have only small capital gains to offset, it may make sense to start your tax-loss harvesting on the bond side of your portfolio.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams. The views should not be considered to be legal or tax advice. The tax rules are complicated, and their impact on a particular individual may differ depending on the individual’s specific circumstances. Views are subject to revision over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Crocs: Aggressive Deleveraging And A 12% FCF Yield
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Shailja Sharma as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The wall street currently viewing Crocs, Inc. (CROX) like a failing business. Investors castigate the stock heavily because sales at HeyDude keep dropping. However, focusing only on weak spot misses the bigger picture and highly profitable reality that Crocs is a cash making machine trading at a high discount.

My Strong buy thesis is straightforward: The main driver for the stock over the next 12-24 months would not be explosive revenue growth but a rapid debt reduction. Based on a forward Free Cash Flow (FCF) yield of over 12%, management is rapidly paying back their $1.23 billion in net debt. They plan on a 1.0x net debt to EBITDA ratio by the end of 2026. As the debt lower, hundreds of millions of dollars are redirected into buying back up to $750 million of their stock.

When you combine this aggressive buyback potential with projected positive 23% operating margins, CROX's current valuation of just 5.7x EV/EBITDA is fundamentally mispriced compared to peers like Deckers and Nike.

To truly understand the Crocs discount, we must look at the competitive scene. The market is pricing Crocs as a "here today, gone tomorrow," while giving high end valuations to its competitors like Deckers (parent of HOKA and UGG). Deckers commands a 20x Forward P/E because it is viewed as a high-growth, premium athletic brand. Crocs is viewed as a periodic trend.

However, a qualitative look at Crocs' growth drivers deconstruct this "short lived trend" narrative. First, Crocs has successfully monetized personalization through its "Jibbitz". These small accessories cost cents to make but sell for dollars which means high margins, making more money than traditional shoemakers. To quantify this growth: in fiscal year 2025, Jibbitz charms accounted for approximately 8% of the total Crocs brand business, generating an estimated $266 million in highly profitable and recurring revenue. Second, the key Crocs brand is seeing explosive international adoption, specially in Asia. Crocs is not in competition with Nike for running shoe business, but it have sustainable competitive advantage in the "casual comfort" segment.

Deleveraging Frees Mearly $700 million Annual FCF:

The true engine of this investment idea is the elite amount of free cash generation. For 2025, the company will generate roughly $659 million in free cash flow. This equals a massive 16% yield compared to the total value of the company. Rather than hoarding this money, management is actively deploying it to fix their balance sheet.

After the HeyDude acquisition, debt was a huge hurdle. Now, that net debt has already fallen, and the company is aggressively paying off its most expensive loans (Term Loan B). By paying $128 million alone in 2025, CROX is on a glide path to carrying very little debt to EBITDA by late 2026.

This is the exact inflection point the market is missing. As debt obligation vanish, that $700 million+ in yearly free cash flow goes directly toward making the equity more valuable. Management still has $750 million to buy back shares. During their recent earnings call, leadership explicitly noted their "powerful value creation model" has already allowed them to buy back and retire about 10% of all their stock. Buying back even more shares at today's cheap prices will naturally force their Earnings Per Share (EPS) higher over the next two years.

Core Margins Remain Elite Despite the HeyDude Drag

The bear argument centers heavily on HeyDude, which is projected to see a 14% drop in revenue in 2025. Yet, even with this anchor, Crocs' overall profitability is exceptionally robust.

The company posted a 22.3% adjusted operating margin for 2025, and expects that to grow modestly to ~23% in 2026. This is largely because massive shift toward direct to consumer sales is being fueled by specific international momentum. Specifically, full-year FY25 international revenues increased by 11.9% year-over-year to $1.61 billion for the core Crocs brand. Furthermore, they keep strict discipline on inventory, which protects their profit margins of positive 37% even when shoppers are demanding heavy discounts.

Valuation: A Glaring Discount to Peers

When we compare Crocs against its competitors, the price makes no sense.

CROX currently trading at roughly around 6.3x forward P/E and a low 5.7x EV/EBITDA. This is way below than half of its historical 12x average. Even more, it is a 40% to 60% discount compared to its peers. Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) trades at roughly 20x P/E, Nike, Inc. (NKE) is near 12x P/E, and Skechers U.S.A, Inc. (SKX) (soon to be privately held since it was acquired by 3G Capital) holds a 10x P/E. The market is pricing Crocs as if its business model is permanently down, but the cash flow proves otherwise.

To Prove The Rationalization of $140 Price Target

To prove this massive discount is real, we have to look beyond basic P/E ratios and do the calculation. Lets built a standard cash flow model to find the true value of the company. Here are the safe and conservative numbers used to reach $140 target:

Sales Growth: I am only modeling a conservative 5% growth in sales next year, dropping to 2% after that and assuming the HeyDude brand stays flat or struggles, while the main Crocs brand carries the weight.

Profit Margins: Let's kept the profit margin steady at 23%, not assuming any cost-cutting or business improvements.

The Risk Buffer (Discount Rate): We applied a strict 9.0% "penalty" to our calculation. This will act as a safety buffer to the risk of a stocks volatility or changing consumer tastes.

Long-Term Future: We assume the company only grows by just 2.5% a year in the future.

Even using these highly conservative, unforgiving numbers, the sheer amount of cash Crocs generates proves the stock is truly worth $140 per share. That means the stock has roughly 45% upside from current level.

Methodology Weakness: It is crucial to note that this math relies densely on long-term growth estimates. If the HeyDude acquisition completely fails to settle and drags the company down, long-term cash flows will recoil, which would significantly lower the $140 price target.

The Bear Case

What Could Go Wrong: To remain objective, I must admit this turnaround have severe risks. The primary threat is that HeyDude's decline cannot be cyclical but structural. If the brand loses its remaining wholesale partners in 2026, the losses could wipe out all the profit from the core Crocs business.

Furthermore, a tough economy is a major threat. Clogs are not a required purchase. If inflation or a weak job market crushes the spending power of average consumers, Crocs might have to drop prices just to clear out their warehouses. If free cash flow drops because of price cuts, management has to stop buying back of equity. Without those equity buyback, the primary catalyst for this thesis collapses and potentially drive the stock down toward $50 per share.

Investor Takeaway

Crocs is not a stock for investors who want a smooth ride or faint of heart, and fixing HeyDude will demand patience. However, it offers a highly attractive setup and good risk reward setup. The market has priced in the worst possible case scenario for the company's revenue, while completely ignored the massive cash they generate and the upcoming wave of stock buyback. To address the timeliness of this investment, we must weigh the near-term catalysts. The primary upward catalyst is the ongoing aggressive repurchase of equity program funded by FCF, which will boost EPS. On the other hand, the primary downward catalyst is the structural risk of HeyDude losing further wholesale partners, dragging down companies margins. Based on the strength of the buybacks counteracting the HeyDude drag, I am setting a near-term price target of $140 within a 10 to 15-month timeframe. I personally think Investors should buy the stock at these cheap levels, relying on the aggressive debt repayment as the main timeline to see considerably good returns over the next 12 to 24 months.

data.title: Barclays: Interesting Fundamentals, But Challenges Are Being Underestimated
data.description:
I recently covered Lloyd's (LYG), another strong contender for quality banks found in the United Kingdom. In this article, I'll be tackling an analysis using similar and supplemental research for Barclays PLC (BCS), with the native ticker BARC. As usual, I do recommend investing natively (BARC in this case) where possible, but the company's NYSE ticker is a very liquid one - it means it can be easily invested in without much worry for liquidity, like we usually need to look at for a non-US company.

Barclays seems to be a well-loved bank at this time. Seeking Alpha, Wall Street, and Quant all have this bank at a "Buy" rating - especially since the material deterioration in share price/valuation that we've seen since about mid-February 2026. We've recovered a bit, but the overall stance is that this is a good play.

In this article, I will give you a from-the-ground-up look at Barclays, what I see in terms of overall appeal, and why I might not see the overall upside that other analysts see - at least not as big as they see it. While Barclays is fundamentally improved from the troubles it once had, I see the market as perhaps underestimating some of the risks the company comes with. This means that I view the company's price targets as lower than other analysts do.

At Wolf of Value, I cover other financials and banks similar to Barclays that I consider to be more attractive than this one, as well as other interesting investments for both conservative, income-oriented investors and more growth-oriented investors.

To say that Barclays' 20-year history is anything but fraught with challenges would be, in my view, a misrepresentation of the last two decades. If you had invested back during the GFC, you'd still be at negative return numbers today, even with all of the dividends that Barclays paid. It's therefore up to Barclays to convince us as investors that its losing streak (such as between 2013-2016) is over and we're now able to see long-term growth.

For the time being, I remain somewhat unconvinced - and here's why.

Barclays PLC - Attractive at the right price, but not necessarily now

There's a difference between a company being cheap and a company being attractively valued for growth. When I invest, I focus on two things. First off, the price target - that's the price I want to buy at/below in order to get an attractive rate of return. But that rate of return is calculated to a fair value target, above or at which I will be relatively disinclined to own the company's shares. This value-oriented approach is what guides me. It's why I say that Barclays was attractive a few years back, and I wish I had invested when I invested in other banks as well. However, my focus at the time was on other financial institutions, and Barclays was not one I focused on.

There were reasons for this - Barclays is far from a trouble-free bank.

It's fair to call the bank as one in "transition," and one that has been here for some time. During the years of post-GFC and 2021, I would argue that Barclays was one of the least attractive banks in the UK - and I do not say that lightly. There were reasons. It started with what became known as the LIBOR scandal in 2012. The bank even had to pay nearly half a billion dollars in fines to because of this.

So, it's not, by my estimate, insignificant concerns that historically have been impacting earnings trends and have caused volatility. It's also not necessarily something where I find myself prepared to say it's now all over; from now on it's all sunshine and roses - I would consider myself a bit more careful than that. My capacity as an investor comes down to pricing risk and finding an appealing value for at-risk assets. Barclays, in retrospect, had attractive pricing some years ago (and I even saw that back then - I just thought other companies were better).

So, fundamentally, in 2026, (as opposed to before, historically), we're talking about a good bank. It has a market capitalization of close to £60B, and it has a BBB+ credit rating. Where it "lacks" is the yield, which is not even 2% at the time of writing, and half that of a competitively priced treasury bond is not an easy sell for me in this market.

Its 2025 results, which were published in mid February (also had something to do with the decline), saw a good meeting of guidance. The company's numbers all came in "mostly in line," with a good CET-1 (14.3%, not that high for a European bank), with most of its KPIs hitting or exceeding the previously stated targets. Cost/income, which is one of the main things I look at, is still high at over 61%, but it's going lower.

The company's NPLs are higher than those of other banks I cover - the LLR (Loan loss rate) is at about 52 bps, which is almost 5x higher than some Swedish banks, but it's also a different operating environment.

The savings and targets the company managed were good. Part of what bugs me is the way the company distributes its capital to shareholders. While there would easily be times when I could say that buybacks were a good idea relative to valuation, the last year has not been one of those. Rather, it has not been one of those times unless you believe in significantly longer-term upside for the company's earnings, which I do not believe as easily as some analysts.

Buybacks done have supported the rising (in my view, at times overvalued) share price and mechanically increased/been a part of the rising results.

The buybacks the bank made have not only mechanically impacted EPS but, due to how ROTE is calculated, also impacted KPIs such as this. This is because ROTE is a ratio measurable to tangible capital. When the company buys back shares, it lowers the equity, which means that the resulting percentage of ROTE goes up.

I argue, therefore, that while results are solid, because they are, they're not as good as the company - or even the market - would have you believe. £2.5B in buybacks would likely have lowered ROTE to about 10.8-10.9% (based on its 2025 buybacks). That means that my stance is that Barclays would not have reached 2025 ROTE targets sans buybacks. And the bank is not done with this, having targeted £15B in shareholder returns until 2028.

I'm obviously not against shareholder returns - or even buybacks - but you, as an investor, need to understand what the company is doing to get where it's going.

In this case, I do not believe the picture is quite as good as the bank would have us believe - and I believe also that the forecast increases this bullish assumption somewhat.

It does not mean that Barclays is a bad investment, but it does mean that I believe the market is not sufficiently legitimizing the impacts of cost-cutting issues (the investing segment cost actually rose 5% due to comps/other), the slimming down of its segments (such as selling off German consumer finance), and other high overhead issues.

If someone were to ask me why I'm not as positive as other analysts on Barclays, it would come down to two things.

First, the bank is too expensive - by which I mean relative to its overheads, which are significantly above those of other European banks that I cover. I also believe the market needs to discount for some of the historical issues the bank has been having, and if you allow the valuation or premiumization for this without a discount, you're not taking the bank's history into consideration.

So where should the company trade?

Barclays Valuation - Upside at a valuation, but not this valuation

Barclays averages, on a 20-year basis, about 9.5x P/E. This valuation is tricky because the bank does bounce up and down a lot in earnings, which colors its valuation averages. However, it also misses quite a lot.

While none of these issues make the bank in any way uninvestable, they all, in my view, need to be discounted for. So what we have are overheads (high), historical issues (uncertain), and forecast uncertainties. When taking into account all of these issues, and Barclays maintaining an investment bank (which other banks have divested), the underlying logic for a market discount for the business becomes clearer.

The current upside for the bank is predicated on Barclays managing significant EPS growth. The current analyst average target is (from 17 analysts) an average of about 18.2% per year until 2028E.

How?

From what I can read, it seems to be a combination of very aggressive share buybacks, cost-income efficiency, and better hedges for NIM/margins and interest.

I view every single one of these prospects as suspect, and here's why.

First off, the buybacks only mechanically increase the company's KPIs. At these valuations, significantly above the five-year average of 7-8x P/E, I view them as an inefficient use of capital - the whole "lipstick on a porcine," without calling Barclays that, of course - it has solid operations. But that's argument one.

Argument two, the cost efficiencies. While I do believe that Barclays will manage to improve its C/I, I don't view the improvements as enough. History has proven that costs in Barclays are harder to lower than one might expect.

If I were to break the expected 15-19% AEPS growth down as per Barclays' estimate, I would say that because of the £15B targeted in buybacks, 40-50% of the company's improvements are to come from buybacks. Because I don't view them as valid, what remains is NIM/margin expansion and income growth/efficiencies. I wouldn't put those above 5-6% in total.

How do I get there?

Barclays itself has revenue growth at above 5% or above. Therefore, if you exclude buybacks and assume that rev growth goes to earnings, actual EPS growth is only 5%. And for Barclays, it's "jaws," meaning its cost-to-income is worse than that of other banks. I argue that a 5% revenue won't amount to 5% EPS - that's not even taking into consideration that its investment bank assets have (and are to remain, according to the bank's own targets), flat at about £200B. If the company is unwilling to take risks, where is the growth coming from, if not financial engineering in the form of buybacks?

In short, I remain unconvinced about an above-5% AEPS growth for this company. I, in fact, put the company at 5% until 2028E, and use the 20-year average to forecast it.

This puts me at a PT of £3.3/share for the bank for a 15% annualized upside for the bank, for a PT that I consider attractive enough. What this means to me is that investors who bought the bank cheap (below £3 or thereabouts) would be wise to at least consider what to do with their holding, if and when Barclays fails to materialize the sort of upside they expect.

I view the current estimates for Barclays as far too optimistic and honestly surprised more analysts have not looked at the buyback impact on ROTE and other KPIs - or look at alternative growth rates, excepting these impacts.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

Barclays is an interesting bank at a low price, given its challenges in overheads, margins, and historical issues (which may, of course, at this time, be past). Based on the company's estimate accuracy, fundamentals, and yield, I would put a £3.3/share price target to clear my hurdle of 15% annualized, which the company is not doing. This comes to an ADR target of $17.71.

I would, based on the current company share price and estimates, give the company a "Hold" rating and wait until we see a better valuation.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative and well run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills three out of three quality indicators and zero out of two valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of £3.3/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's not attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Lightwave Logic: Strong Technical Optionality, But Adoption Is A Big Hurdle
data.description:
Lightwave Logic (LWLG) has been on an absolute roll in the last year, with shares already 10x from the same time last year. The company has a very promising product in theory, but for now, it still has a long way to go before commercialization is a reality. The lower power and higher efficiency, as well as not stripping out the current SiPho technology, but integrating within it, should be very appealing for every player in the AI space; however, there is still a long way to go and many questions that need to be answered. I will try to answer some of the concerns regarding the company that I think will be quite useful for everyone who is not an expert in the technology, me included, and is looking for an investment idea.

What is the technology behind the company?

LWLG is currently a pre-commercial technology platform that is developing what are called electro-optic polymers for photonic devices. This technology, in plain English, is supposed to help data move faster and with less power in the internet infrastructure. Currently, the company’s business model is centered on developing the technology, validating it with customers and partners, and eventually monetizing it through licensing, material supply, and engineering-related work, rather than mass product sales.

The idea at the core is to make optical communication components, like modulators, much more efficient by using its proprietary polymer materials that are intended to work with existing photonics platforms like silicon photonics, or SiPho, and indium phosphide, or InP. This proposition immediately sounds a lot more appealing than a company that comes up with a different method altogether because, through their research, they found it to be much more efficient and cheaper than the current process. LWLG is not trying to replace what is already proven to be a good way to reach scale, but rather to integrate with the current process, which, if proven to be scalable with future partnership and further research, will become a lot more attractive to all the players in the AI data center space.

I am not going to pretend I know what the technology is, or how it works, so I will focus on the company’s prospects as an investment rather than what the technology is, and how promising it is in theory.

Why did the stock shoot up so much?

The company’s share price did not 10x overnight. It did happen relatively quickly, though, in just a year. The combination of different milestones meant that the market has repriced LWLG because it moved from a pure science story toward something that appears to be quite achievable and integrated into real-world applications.

Back in 2025, one of the first important steps came from the management aggressively pursuing expansion, with technical partnerships around high-speed optical links for AI and data center use cases. The company expanded its partnership with Polariton in March 2025 to accelerate the introduction of 400Gb/s per lane, specifically for AI and data center optical links. At this point, the company was still being valued purely on optionality. The market seemed to think it was a neat idea, but lacking any viable proof that it could be more than just a neat idea.

The bigger moves came in late 2025, when LWLG announced a technical program with its second Fortune Global 500 partner, to co-develop 400GB/s co-packaged optics and to produce a Process Design Kit, or a PDK. In SiPho, a PDK is the foundry-provided set of design rules, component models, and simulation files, which allow engineers to design chips that can be manufactured in that process. This was a critical stage for the company because its technology can now be linked specifically to the AI growth story and potential. The technology seems to be designable within the current workflow.

After that, the company continued moving through a design-win cycle, which can be seen as a major psychological change because it seems that the platform was starting to look a lot more viable in real-life applications, rather than being tested in isolation.

The biggest moves came in 2026. Lightwave announced massive partnerships with well-known foundries, like GlobalFoundries (GFS), and with Tower Semiconductor (TSEM) to enable high-speed, lower-power modulators on TSEM’s SiPho platform. Since then, the company’s share price continued to advance, finally reaching 10x in just one year. It appears that the market validated the company’s foundry credibility.

How is LWLG going to make money?

It appears that the company is going to look to make money in three ways: technology transfer/partnerships with foundries and other device makers, material sales, and licensing/royalties.

The company doesn’t have a full high-volume semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem, so it needs to work with foundries like the ones mentioned earlier, so it can scale through already-established partners. It doesn’t want to build its own fabs and process stacks. So, if the partnership ends up using LWLG’s technology to augment within the current SiPho process, the company will make money on upfront development or engineering work, licensing fees for the technology, material supply agreements, and royalty streams if the partner ships the product that has LWLG’s technology inside it. So, what we can gather from this is that partnerships are at the forefront of importance for the technology to be commercially viable, and the success of the company overall.

This type of model also means that, once proven to be commercially viable at scale and appealing all around, the company’s profitability will be very high. Just looking at the company’s latest full-year financials, we can see that net sales were around $237k, while its cost of sales was only around $7k. This means that its gross margins are around 97%. That is absurd, and I don’t think we can take that as the final long-term gross margin, because we do not know how the company is going to perform at scale yet. However, since the model is very asset-light and focuses on design wins and IP, I can see how the company could achieve significant operating leverage.

How long until we see real revenues?

This is one of the tougher ones to answer, in my opinion. The current bottleneck is not demand interest alone. It is also qualification and productization. The company still needs to be a lot more aggressive to get the technology in as many hands as possible, and those partners need to go through prototype, testing, and, if all goes well, foundry integration and final production. We cannot trust any of the revenue that we saw because, first, it was minimal, and second, more than half of that was actually non-recurring engineering revenue. The good part is that around $100k was already licensing/royalty revenue.

In the latest conference call, the management said that they don’t expect to see meaningful revenues until 2027 at the earliest. And that is also not even set in stone.

What do the financials look like right now?

It is essential to look at the company’s financials, especially when the company is not making very much in terms of revenue. Capital is necessary in these situations to keep afloat until the product goes from theory to commercialization. So, LWLG finished the year with a massive cash pile of around $69 million, which increased from around $26.7 million last year. Additionally, the company has no LT debt, which is vital because all the capital it has on hand will be used to continue the research on the viability of the product, as well as keeping the company alive. This past year, the company burned through almost $14 million in cash, and had -$15 million in free cash flow, which means that if the company doesn’t get another injection of capital through a share offering or another deal, its runway sits at around 4.6 years if we take the company’s negative free cash flow. I think that is excellent. It gives plenty of time to see if the product will be viable to be inserted in the current SiPho process and has the ability to be scaled.

Main concerns and takeaways

I think the main concerns I still have are not the technology, because clearly, according to the company, it is a lot more efficient and powerful. On paper. The technology still has to move from promising lab/device results to consistent, manufacturable production at scale. The partnerships with the two foundries paint a very good picture right now, which helped its share price skyrocket, but these are just the beginnings of real-world testing efforts to see if its technology is indeed easy to integrate and viable at commercial scale. Not only is viability at scale needed, but reliability is also key in this situation. The design has not been proven yet to be reliable at scale. Can it be produced at high yields? That remains to be a big question.

Another adoption barrier is the customers. The buyers of photonics already have their manufacturing process down to where it is reliable, not too expensive, or even quite cheap. The whole process is smooth and has been working for years. The new design that integrates with SiPho still has to go through a long qualification process cycle, and even if it is superior, the company has to justify the cost and risks of switching to this new process. It has to be far superior to the current process for companies to start paying attention.

So, there are risks, and there are plenty of positives when looking at Lightwave Logic. I see it right now as a high-optionality commercialization story that has plenty of runway to get it done but is still cloaked with a lot of uncertainty. It also seems like the market has priced in quite a bit of the positives, since a share price appreciation of 1000%+ is not typical for any company, especially for such a speculative one like this one. If you were smart enough to get in on the action when it was trading under $1 and didn’t sell after it went up to $5, what is the point in selling it right now? Just ride the AI wave hype and maybe withdraw your initial investment and keep holding the rest. Who knows what is going to happen to the company? The next 12-24 months will be crucial. A make-or-break situation. For now, I will observe from afar.

data.title: Graco's Valuation Leaves Little Room For Error
data.description:
One of the stocks that has been on my watchlist for a long time is the industrial company Graco Inc. (GGG). However, I always considered the stock a little too expensive to be a good investment. This is including my last article about the company, which was published shortly before Christmas 2025. In the conclusion of my last article, I wrote:

At this point, we can argue that Graco is almost fairly valued and trading close to its intrinsic value. Nevertheless, I will remain cautious and considering that a margin of safety should be built into every investment, I would wait for either Graco’s stock price decline again (at least about 20%) or for the business to improve significantly over the next few quarters (with the stock price remaining in its current range).

And although analysts are more optimistic with price targets, I would rather stay on the side of caution and will wait until the stock declines at least below the lower range of my orange marked intrinsic value range.

Since my last article was published, the stock has increased by about 4.5% (about 5.0% when including dividends). The stock outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500), which declined about 1% in the same timeframe. However, in the following article, I will argue once again that Graco is a Hold at best. Despite being a great business, it is trading for high valuation multiples, and the risks are probably increased due to the war with Iran.

Last Results

We start by looking at the full-year results and net sales increased from $2,113.3 million in fiscal 2024 to $2,236.6 million in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 5.8% year-over-year growth. Operating earnings increased 9.6% year-over-year from $570.1 million in the previous year to $624.8 million in fiscal 2025. Finally, diluted net income per share also increased from $2.82 in fiscal 2024 to $3.08 in fiscal 2025 – resulting in 9.2% year-over-year bottom line growth.

While full-year results for Graco were certainly solid, fourth quarter results were even better. Net sales increased from $548.7 million in Q4/24 to $593.2 million in Q4/25 – resulting in 8.1% year-over-year growth. Operating earnings increased 22.0% year-over-year from $130.0 million in the previous quarter to $158.6 million in this quarter. Finally, diluted net income per share increased even 25.4% year-over-year from $0.63 in Q4/24 to $0.79 in Q4/25.

In the case of Graco, we see quarterly revenue growth accelerating in the last few quarters, which is a good sign and currently quarterly revenue growth is above the long-term average of 6.98% again. We also see in the chart above that Graco’s revenue growth is fluctuating heavily, and we also see what a huge negative impact the Great Financial Crisis had on Graco and what a problem the collapsing housing market is for the business.

Currency Effects and Growth by Acquisitions

While these numbers look solid at first glance, we also must look at the contributors for revenue growth. For the full year of fiscal 2025, growth stemmed from currency effects (contributing 1% to overall growth) and especially from acquisitions (which contributed 5% to growth). Volume and price didn’t contribute at all, and therefore organic revenue growth was 0%.

When looking at the fourth quarter, the picture is looking a little bit better, but volume and price also contributed only 2% to revenue growth, while acquisitions contributed 4% and currency effects contributed 2%. In the last two years, Graco made several acquisitions – including Radia for $69 million, Color Service for $63 million or Corob for $230 million.

When a business is acquiring other companies, we should also look at the balance sheet as acquisitions might lead to high debt levels as well as high amounts of goodwill on the balance sheet. However, Graco is making mostly small acquisitions and still has a great balance sheet. On December 26, 2025, the company had no long-term debt and $1.6 million in short-term debt. Compared to a free cash flow of more than $600 million and $624 million in cash and cash equivalents, the debt is negligible.

Graco also has $585.3 million in goodwill on its balance sheet, which is not great news. However, when comparing goodwill to total assets (17.9%) and total equity (22.0%) we should not really be worried about goodwill. The company certainly can continue its strategy and use the cash on its balance sheet and free cash flow to make further acquisitions in the coming quarters.

Growth and Expectations

Acquisitions are also a huge part of management’s long-term growth targets and will contribute about 3% to 4% to total growth.

Management remains optimistic that its business can grow to bottom line at least 12% annually through a cycle and analysts are similarly optimistic for the next few years. Until fiscal 2030, analysts are expecting earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 11.1%. Now we can point out that estimates for fiscal 2029 and fiscal 2030 are based only on one (rather optimistic) analyst. But even for the next three years, analysts are still optimistic and assume high single-digit growth rates.

However, for fiscal 2026, management is much more pessimistic and assumes only low single-digit growth rates. As always, Graco is providing a detailed outlook for the business and environment, but the picture is looking not so great.

During the last earnings call, Graco commented:

Both the industrial and expansion market segments grew organically for the full year, and we are optimistic about the growth in contract during the fourth quarter. We're also pleased with the performance and contributions made by COROB, Color Service and Radia this year, and we're hopeful that we will continue to see actionable opportunities in 2026. Graco has engaged employees that are focused on our key initiatives of product innovation, pursuing strategic acquisitions and advancing the One Graco operating model. We're offering 2026 revenue guidance of low single-digit organic growth on a constant currency basis and mid-single-digit growth after factoring in expected incremental sales from the Color Service and Radia acquisitions.

Graco and Oil Prices

The last material we have for Graco (earnings release, presentation and earnings call) does not reflect the conflict in the Middle East in any way. While there were already fears the conflict might escalate, it was not yet obvious and is probably not reflected in any guidance or statements. But with the oil price being extremely volatile and at least 50% higher compared to January 2026 when Graco issued its guidance, the outlook might be a little different at this point.

Graco’s business could be hit in two different ways by higher oil prices. On the one hand, Graco must ship its products from the United States to other countries around the world and is therefore dependent on fuel prices and freight prices, which will probably rise in the coming months. On the other, a lot of Graco’s products are petrochemical-based and with rising oil prices the costs for raw materials will also be higher.

I have already read the argument that Graco’s industrial segment might profit from the situation as demand for high-pressure valves and fluid management systems might be higher. Due to the huge amounts of free cash flow oil companies are generating right now and will generate in the coming months, these companies might increase capital expenditures and spend more cash on maintaining rigs and pipelines.

In my opinion, this argument doesn’t make much sense. First, I rather assume the additional cash will be used for special dividends or share buybacks and probably not for additional CapEx. Second, when looking at net sales by end market, oil & natural gas is making up only 2% of Graco’s sales – and this is certainly not enough to have a huge impact on Graco’s overall results.

Instead, the most important end market is construction, which is making up 52% of total sales and a struggling housing and construction market will have a much bigger impact. During the last earnings call, management also issued a rather cautious guidance for the United States housing market:

But I think there's a sense that at least here in North America, kind of a flattish outlook again on residential housing. So not like any kind of a dramatic shift there. As I said in my opening comments, being held back a little bit by affordability.

In the last few years, growth rates for construction spending declined and in the last few months, construction spendings even declined.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must look at the intrinsic value and determine whether Graco is a good investment. At the time of writing, Graco is trading for 29 times earnings, which certainly does not seem cheap. However, Graco is also trading for 23.7 times free cash flow, and this must be seen as reasonable for a company that is usually growing with a solid pace. The stock is clearly trading below the 10-year average P/FCF ratio of 33.21.

Simple valuation multiples can only provide us with a first hint whether a stock might be overvalued or a good investment. A much better way is to determine an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we are calculating with a discount rate of 10% (the annual return we like to achieve at least) and the last reported number of diluted shares outstanding (168.1 million in Q4/25). In a first calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters as a basis for our calculation, which was $638 million. And in an optimistic assumption we can calculate with 10% growth for the next ten years – more or less in line with Graco’s own long-term expectations. In 10 years from now, we calculate with 4% growth till perpetuity (in line with CFI’s recommendations for a terminal growth rate). Calculating with these assumptions leads to an intrinsic value of $94.31 for Graco and the stock might still be slightly undervalued.

However, we must question whether the free cash flow of the last four quarters is a reasonable assumption for our calculation. In the last four quarters, Graco reported a free cash flow margin of 28.5% - the highest margin at least since the late 1990s (I don’t have data for the time before). In the last ten years, the free cash flow margin was 18.6% and over the long run, the free cash flow margin was 17.0%. And while it is certainly reasonable to assume an improving free cash flow margin, but when looking at the chart above, the current free cash flow seems like an outlier. Instead, we should be more cautious – especially when considering the current risk we mentioned above – and assume only a free cash flow margin of 20%. This leads to a free cash flow of $447 million, which we can take as basis in our calculation.

When calculating with these metrics, we get an intrinsic value of $66.08 for Graco, and the stock is still overvalued at this point.

Upcoming Quarter

On April 22, 2026, Graco will report first quarter results for fiscal 2026. Analysts (on average) expect revenue to be $560.87 million on average. This would result in 6.2% year-over-year growth for Graco. Earnings per share are expected to be either $0.74 (adjusted EPS) or $0.75 (GAAP numbers). This would result in 1.4% year-over-year growth for the bottom line.

In the last 90 days, four analysts raised estimates, while no single analyst lowered estimates in the same timeframe. And when looking at earnings revisions, it does not seem like analysts got more pessimistic in the last two months – despite the risks and challenges for Graco mentioned above.

I do not expect Graco to report worse numbers than analysts are expecting and the above-mentioned risks probably won’t have a huge negative impact on first quarter results. But we should pay close attention to management’s comments during the earnings call and especially look out for statements about increased risks and how raw material prices and supply chain issues might have a negative impact on the company in the next few quarters. And I also think it is possible that management will lower its guidance a little bit and maybe will get at least more cautious in the words it is using.

Conclusion

Graco clearly remains a Hold at this point, although the business has a wide economic moat and is a great company. But it is also true that Graco is usually heavily affected during recessions (see the quarterly revenue decline of 38% during the Great Financial Crisis in the chart above) and with the economy remaining very fragile, the high oil prices as well as disturbed supply chains create a major risk for the economy. In such an environment, I would rather stay on the sidelines – especially for a business that is still trading for close to 30 times earnings and is heavily affected by recessions.

In the case of Graco, I would not rule out that the stock might decline to its 2022 lows around $60 once again over the next few months. With the upside potential being limited here, I still don’t think the stock is a Buy.

data.title: FSCO: PIK's Are The Industry Default Mechanism - Strong Sell
data.description:
The FS Credit Opportunities Corp. (FSCO) Is a non-diversified and closed-end management investment company that seeks to generate total return consisting of a high level of current income and capital appreciation, with a secondary objective of capital preservation.

The fund is approximately $2.2 billion in AUM, focused on event-driven credit, special situations, private capital, and lower and core middle market private credit. The portfolio is weighted toward senior secured first lien loans to borrowers with EBITDA in the $25 million to $75 million range per the most recent annual report. I am initiating FSCO as a Strong Sell on Seeking Alpha. The discount to NAV trade that I was initially looking at is being overwhelmed by what is sitting inside of the investments of the funds, the broader private credit cycle, and an income statement that is approx. 22% payment-in-kind (PIK) interest against an industry average of 7-8%,. As someone who started in fixed income, this is a credit pick-apart instead of a discount story.

Price to NAV

FSCO trades at approx. $5/share, against a NAV of approx. $7/share. This is a price/NAV discount of about 30%, which is the widest discount the fund has traded at since the listing in 2022. The fund has reported a YTD price return of –16.1% through the end of March, against a +1.7% NAV Total Return for the same time frame. The board reset the monthly distribution from 0.0678 to 0.0583 in March of 2026, which is a 14% cut that Andrew Beckman (the Portfolio Manager) has framed as a response to floating-rate yield compression after the 75 bps in rate cuts that the fed had conducted in 2025. The cut closed a coverage gap that had already existed. Additionally, the price of convergence between NAV and price per share is a good conversation point to start at, as the market does not seem to believe in the growth of the underlying NAV in the fund. After going through all the holdings of this fund, I believe the market is right.

Private Credit and Macro Backdrop

My setup for a strong sell starts with what is happening within this subsection of the alternative investment asset class. Fitch's January 2026 update on the U.S. Private Credit Default Rate displays the PCDR at 5.8% on a TTM basis, the highest since the index's beginning in August 2024. The Privately Monitored Rating component sits at 9.4%, and Fitch recorded 11 default events in February 2026, nearly double the 2025 monthly average of 5.9. An important detail that matters the most to me is that 11 of the February default events, 7 out of 11 involved the introduction of payment-in-kind (PIK) interest in lieu of cash interest. PIK conversion is now the dominant default mechanism Fitch is observing across the market.

Additionally, Morgan Stanley raised its direct lending default forecast to 8% in mid-march, citing AI software disruption and looming maturity walls. UBS sees up to 15% in a worst case, up from 13% just 3 weeks earlier. The historical norm is 2-2.5%. The market seems to price in a 3-7x normalization of defaults over 12-18 months. Sector mix matters as well. Consumer product defaults rose to 12.8% in January of 2026, which more than doubled from 6.1% in the prior year. Healthcare services have the highest unique defaulter count within the PC space.

Also, the redemption side is going through turmoil. Apollo capped redemptions in one of the semi-liquid PC funds in late March; Ares followed one day later, capping the $10.7 billion strategic income fund at the 5% quarterly limit after withdrawal requests surged to 11.6%. Blue Owl had already capped two of their PC funds at 5% in February after being asked to pull ~22% in Q1 and permanently halted quarterly redemptions on OBDC II. BDC sales have also fallen 40% in early 2026, and the S&P BDC index is down by about 15% YTD. So, the retail capital that funded the expansion of PC in 2024 and 2025 is shrinking. This is just the macro background for FSCO. Holdings are next.

The PIK Income Stack

The most important number in the FSCO annual report is buried within the consolidated statement of operations (Item 1 of the N-CSR). From this filing, the fund recorded $51.5 million in PIK interest income in 2025 against total investment income of $235.3 million. PIK is approx. 21.9% of the entire income statement. Public BDCs receive, on average, about of 7-8% of investment income via PIK. FSCO is running levels at about 3x the industry average.

A Discussion on PIKs – And why they are important to consider on FSCO

For readers who do not find themselves traditionally involved in PC, PIK (payment-in-kind) interest is a coupon mechanic where the borrow pays interest by issuing more debt to the lender instead of paying off via cash. For example, if a $100 million loan carries 12% APR, at the end of the year the borrower owes $112 million instead of $100mm, and the lender records $12 million of “interest income” on the books, even though no income was materially received. Additionally, the principal compounds against the borrower. The accrual flows through the income statement, but the cash flow statement never sees it.

PIK loans do exist for legit reasons. Growth stage companies with negative FCF, but real EV sometimes utilize PIKs to preserve liquidity through a buildout phase, with an expectation that cash flow will inflect later and cover the accrued balance at refinancing (or sale). Mezzanine and second lien tranches in LBOs often carry a PIK toggle option on the loan covenant as a feature, allowing the borrower to elect to pay cash or PIK based on compliance with the covenant. When used in moderation, PIK is a useful tool, but the problem is when the tool becomes a common structure.

There are 3 reasons why PIK matters disproportionately more for credit analysis. PIK income has no cash conversion until the principal is repaid or refinanced. A fund can report a fully covered distribution on a tax basis while its actual cash inflows fall well short of cash outflows. The shortfall then gets papered over by the existing cash balances, new borrowings, or selling other portfolio positions; None of which are sustainable. Additionally, PIK accruals inflate the principal balance, which means the fund mark on the position is rising in dollar terms even when the underlying borrower is deteriorating. A loan that started at $50 million at 13% PIK is at approximately $72 million of accrued principal 2 years later. If the borrower can only support an enterprise value supporting $50 million of debt at exit, the realized loss is $22 million on a position that was carried out the entire time. Last, PIKs are the precursor to non-accrual designation. Lenders convert cash coupons to PIK when the borrower runs out of cash. Once the PIK accrual itself becomes non-collectible, the position moves to non-accrual, and then the previous accrued income reverses through a realized loss. PIK conversion is a leading indicator, and non-accrual is a lagging indicator of the same underlying deterioration of credit.

Accounting treatment is important to discuss, too. Under ASC 946 and the AICPA Investment Companies guide, PIK interest is recognized as income on the accrual basis if the position is performing, with the accrued PIK added to the cost basis of the investment. When the position moves to non-accrual, the previously accrued PIK is written off on the income statement, but the principal on the accrued PIK remains on the schedule of investments, amortized, until the workout. This is why FSCO can report a 3% non-accrual rate at fair value with a higher impairment rate, at amortized costs. The marks have been working off the PIK accruals as they happen.

For taxes, PIK income is taxable of the fund (and the shareholders via RIC pass-through structure) in the year it is accrued, even if no cash is collected. This creates a mismatch where the fund must distribute taxable income to maintain RIC status while the actual cash collections lag. This gap is filled by leverage, by selling positions, or issuing new shares, none of which add value to NAV, when the discount is already wide. Now, onto the holdings of FSCO.

Top 10 Holdings: A Quarter Of The Fund In Cash

Per the most recent disclosed top 10 holdings, the FSCO book looks like this:

First, approx. 27.9% of the fund is in cash and Treasuries. That is enormous for a leveraged credit CEF and effectively means only 72% of NAV is deployed into the credit strategy investors are paying a 7.9% expense ratio for. The fund is collecting roughly 3.7% on the Treasury holding and money market yields on the $276 million of cash, against credit spreads of S+500 to S+1000+ on the deployed book The opportunity cost is approximately 3-4% per year on 28% of NAV, which translates to roughly 100 bps of return drag annually that the manager has chosen to accept rather than deploy into a stressed market (which isn’t necessarily a bad thing). The good read on this is that Beckman is being patient before entering/deploying liquid capital. The bad read is that the fund cannot find deployable opportunities that are risk-adjusted and instead are hiding behind the cash buffer that masks compressed income. I subscribe to the latter.

Additionally, of the 8 non cash positions (approx. 25% of NAV in actual deployed credit), 3 are PIK-structured with TCFIII Owl being all-PIK 12%, Accupac being a hybrid S+300 + 6% PIK, and Powerhouse (all PIK, approx. 16%) Neovia carries a PIK toggle allowing the borrower to elect up to 9% PIK instead of cash, currently being paid in cash, but with the toggle as a covenant safety structure. TCFIII Owl Finance is the single largest credit position in the entire fund at 4.45% of NAV, and it is paying zero in cash interest. This alone should cause an investor to pause and reconsider, as the largest single bet in the portfolio is a borrower that the underwriting team marked as unable to service debt in cash.

The schedule of investments specifically flags these with footnote (m) as related party investments where an affiliate of the Fund's investment adviser serves as collateral manager. CLO equity sits at the bottom of the CLO capital stack as the residual claim on a loan pool after all tranches are paid. It is essentially a way to have levered exposure to broadly syndicated loans with a 10%+ upside in good years, and a write down to 0 situations in bad years. FSCO's CLO equity book lost approximately $9.7 million of fair value in 2025, a 25% write down on cost. The fact that an affiliate of the manager originated and manages these vehicles is a related-party disclosure item that I believe deserves more attention than it has received.
