The other 54% is supported by PIK accruals being recycled through repayments and the existing $402.9 million cash buffer. This does not work if defaults rise and repayments stretch, and given the broad PC environment, is a disaster waiting to happen for buyers of FSCO.

Tradability

FSCO trades about 2.9 million shares in a day, and about $15mm in dollar value. Bid-asks are about 1-13 cents, but due to it being $5/share, is about 0.25%-2.6% of price in spread. FSCO also has a very limited options market that is relatively illiquid. With low volume, FSCO IS tradable, but not superb by any means.

Upside Risks

The clear risk that would be probable would involve a board buyback announcement. Future Standard has not been historically an activist on their own discount, but a 30% discount to NAV is enough that a defensive program to buy back shares is plausible to happen. Even a 5-10% buyback would compress the discount by approx. 10 points within days. Additionally, default rates in PC that originate from the aforementioned analyst desks may overshoot what will happen. The third risk involves the 28% cash buffer, which does give Beckman the opportunity to deploy into distressed debt, at distressed prices. If the portfolio strategy works, NAV grows attractively into the discount. I would re-rate FSCO into a sell on a buyback of $50 million or more, or to a hold on the PIK exposure dropping below 18%.

Takeaway

FSCO is a Strong Sell. The discount looks compelling on its own. The 14% market yield also looks compelling on its own. Both lose their appeal once you actually read the schedule of investments. Approx. 22% of reported income is PIK against an industry average of 7-8%. The largest single credit position in the fund, TCFIII Owl Finance, at 4.45% of NAV, is paying zero in cash interest. The all-PIK and hybrid PIK positions total approx. $400 million of principal exposure where cash is being accrued instead of collected. The CLO equity book is a related party trade that has already lost 25% of cost in 2025. The sector mix puts 52% of the portfolio in the four worst performing private credit buckets in the current default cycle. The cash/treasuries position is hiding income compression behind the appearance of large amounts of dry powder. With a closed-end structure that provides no mechanic for discount closure, and the rest of the asset class gating retail redemptions, I expect FSOC to severely underperform over the next year. Harvest the distribution if you currently own it, but I would stay away from FSCO if you don’t have it already.

data.title: Revolution Medicines: PDAC Data Wows Wall Street - Jury's Out On Further Upside
data.description:
Investment Overview

The stock of Revolution Medicines, Inc. (RVMD), is up >40% in early trading today, reaching a value of >$131 (at the time of writing), after the Redwood City, California-based biotech company reported positive data from a Phase 3 study of its drug candidate daraxonrasib in patients with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma ("PDAC") who had been previously treated.

According to a press release issued today:

Daraxonrasib taken orally once daily demonstrated statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvements in progression-free survival ("PFS") and overall survival ("OS") compared with standard of care cytotoxic chemotherapy delivered intravenously.

In the overall (intent-to-treat) study population, daraxonrasib demonstrated a median OS of 13.2 months versus 6.7 months for chemotherapy, with a hazard ratio of 0.40 (p < 0.0001). Daraxonrasib was generally well tolerated, with a manageable safety profile and with no new safety signals.

Management adds that:

Based on the results from this first interim analysis, all PFS and OS endpoint results are considered final. Revolution Medicines intends to submit these data to global regulatory authorities, including to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration as part of a future New Drug Application under a Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher, and for presentation at the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology Annual Meeting.

The Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher ("CNPV") is a new pilot scheme introduced by the FDA as a "pathway to dramatically reduce review times for drug and biological product applications and manufacturing or efficacy supplements," and allows for fast-track drug application reviews of as little as 1-2 months, according to the FDA's website.

This seemingly raises the prospect of Revolution, which completed its IPO in February 2020, raising ~$238m via the issuance of ~14m shares priced at $17 per share, securing its first commercial approval this year. Revolution reported a cash position of ~$2bn as of the end of 2025, so funding a commercial launch ought to present few problems, while PDAC patients have high unmet needs. According to Revolution's press release:

Pancreatic cancer is one of the most lethal malignancies, characterized by its typically late-stage diagnosis, resistance to standard chemotherapy, and high mortality rate. In the U.S., recent estimates indicate that annually approximately 60,000 people will be diagnosed with pancreatic cancer, and about 50,000 people will die from this aggressive disease.

Due to the lack of early symptoms and detection methods, approximately 80% of patients are diagnosed with PDAC at an advanced or metastatic stage. It is the most commonly RAS-addicted of all major cancers, and more than 90% of patients have tumors that harbor RAS mutations.2 Metastatic PDAC remains one of the most common causes of cancer-related deaths in the U.S., with a five-year survival rate of approximately 3%.

Analysis: Revolution Shrugs Off Merck M&A Saga With Resounding Study Win

In September last year, I gave Revolution Medicines stock a Buy rating - my second in a row following a Buy call in December 2024 (the stock is up >170% since) - after shares soared in response to data shared by the company from three Phase 1 studies of daraxonrasib.

Two of the studies were evaluating data from first-line metastatic patients (i.e., patients who had not previously been treated with another drug), but the third was long-term follow-up from patients with second-line and beyond (2L+) metastatic PDAC treated with daraxonrasib 300 mg daily (QD). Highlights from this study were shared as below:

  • The confirmed objective response rate ("ORR") per RECIST v1.1 was 35% and 29%.
  • The disease control rate ("DCR") was 92% and 95%.
  • The median progression-free survival ("PFS") was 8.5 months (95% confidence interval ("CI"), 6.7 – 10.5) and 8.1 months (95% CI, 5.9 – 10.1).
  • The median overall survival ("OS") was 13.1 (95% CI, 10.9 – NE) and 15.6 months (95% CI, 10.9 – NE).
  • Median follow-up was 16.7 months.

As such, Wall Street likely anticipated that the Phase 3 RASolute 302 data would also be positive, and so it has proven, although it is interesting to note that in January, the pharma giant Merck & Co. (MRK), marketer and seller of the world's best-selling cancer med, Keytruda (>$30bn revenues in 2025), apparently pulled out of a deal to acquire Revolution for a sum in the region of $28bn - $30bn.

I discussed Merck's decision in a note shared with Seeking Alpha readers in January, noting that "analysts have suggested that daraxonrasib could achieve peak annual revenues of >$7bn," but also writing that:

we might ask whether Merck insiders truly felt that paying >$25bn to acquire Revolution was necessary, given an already full pipeline addressing five different therapeutic modalities. I count at least ten oncology drugs in Merck's pipeline already.

I concluded that "Merck's proposed acquisition of Revolution feels like a deal that simply does not need to happen," and actually awarded Revolution stock a Sell rating, suggesting that "my feeling is the market may have gotten slightly ahead of itself in its pricing of Revolution," and that sentiment proved true initially, as Revolution stock slipped from ~$107 per share to <$92 per share.

I felt Merck would have completed extensive due diligence on Revolution and perhaps identified some flaws in its portfolio of RAS-targeting products (the full pipeline is shown below, source: corporate presentation), but today's news makes me wonder if Merck has missed out on a good deal after all.

Revolution discusses Daraxonrasib as follows in its 2025 annual report/10-K filing:

Daraxonrasib, our RAS(ON) multi-selective inhibitor, is designed as an oral, RAS-selective tri-complex inhibitor of multiple RAS(ON) variants containing cancer driver mutations at all three of the major RAS mutation hotspot positions, G12, G13, and Q61. Daraxonrasib inhibits all three major RAS isoforms, suppressing the mutant cancer driver and cooperating wild-type RAS proteins.

Rat sarcoma, or RAS proteins, are part of the Ras/Raf/MAPK signaling pathway, and Revolution also notes in its 10-K filing:

Variants in RAS proteins are among the most common oncogenic drivers of cancer. Based on tumor mutation frequencies from Foundation Medicine data, scaled to estimated patient numbers using cancer incidence from the American Cancer Society Cancer Facts and Figures, there are an estimated more than 190,000 new RAS mutant cancer diagnoses each year in the U.S. These include approximately 60,000 patients with NSCLC, representing approximately 30% of NSCLC diagnoses, approximately 75,000 patients with colorectal cancer (CRC), representing approximately 50% of CRC diagnoses, and approximately 56,000 patients with PDAC, representing more than 90% of PDAC diagnoses.

Second-line PDAC treatment may be the smallest of Revolution's target markets, but as mentioned, it is a field of high unmet need, and Revolution may see it as a "beachhead," with what seems like an inevitable approval in this indication followed by approvals in first-line PDAC and potentially non-small cell lung cancer - one of the largest oncology markets.

RAS has proven to be a tricky target for drug developers - so far as I am aware, the only other approved drugs that target RAS are the KRAS ("kirsten rat sarcoma") targeting drugs Lumakras (sotorasib), marketed and sold by Amgen (AMGN), and Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Krazati (adagrasib), but both drugs have been dogged by somewhat underwhelming efficacy and safety data and earned ~$356m and ~$202m last year.

It seems as though Revolution has won over the FDA already, given its receipt of a CNPV, and as mentioned, this means daraxonrasib could be approved this year. Analysts have suggested the drug could earn >$5bn in peak annual revenues in PDAC alone.

Are There Any Clouds On Revolution's Horizon?

With $2bn cash, a drug that clinical study data suggests could revolutionize the way pancreatic cancer - one of the hardest to treat cancers with the worst prognosis for patients - is treated, and with opportunities to secure approvals in first-line PDAC and NSCLC for not one but in fact two or even three different drugs, it's hard to think of many negatives at this time.

Of course, no drug approval is ever guaranteed - New Drug Applications ("NDAs") can be rejected due to manufacturing deficiencies, insufficient data, lack of clarity in trial data, requirements for further studies, etc. However, given the CNPV award, it seems that the FDA is already convinced by the safety and efficacy profile of the drug.

Most of that conviction presumably stems from the long-term monotherapy data shared by Revolution last September, as seemingly we do not yet have all of the data from RASolute 302, i.e., PFS, ORR, DCR, or any detailed safety data. During a fireside chat at the TD Cowen Healthcare conference in March, Revolution's CEO Mark Goldsmith did discuss the 302 study data as follows:

generally, we would like to show data from that trial. It's obviously a very important trial. We're excited about it. It could potentially have far-reaching impact on standards of care for pancreatic cancer. We'd like to be able to share the actual data in a scientific context in a meeting context in which peers can challenge it and review it. But we'll see, let us see the data when it's unblinded, and then we'll be able to make a decision about how to present it.

As such, it seems possible that a more detailed data disclosure could lead to peers questioning the data, although equally, it may confirm the market's current optimism.

In terms of Revolution's market cap valuation today, which stands at ~$26bn, we should perhaps note that an approval in second-line PDAC does not guarantee a follow-on approval in first-line metastatic, although study data in that indication - an ORR of 47% in one 38 patients study - seems encouraging.

Neither does success in PDAC guarantee success in NSCLC, although again, in NSCLC patients with RAS G12X mutations, the drug has achieved a median PFS of 9.8m, and a median OS of 17.7m, significantly superior to current standards of care ("SoCs"), as per the table below (shared by Revolution):

We can also note that Revolution's other drugs are less far advanced than daraxonrasib and target the same markets, so even if they produce strong data and push for approval, they may simply end up cannibalizing sales of Daraxonrasib.

Concluding Thoughts: An Excellent Day For Revolution, But Will Big Pharma Be Tempted To Make A Deal?

In terms of the investment opportunity in play re Revolution stock today, that is less clear than today's strong data readout, in my view. Revolution is now (my research suggests) the most valuable pre-commercial stage pharma, worth more today than, e.g., Biogen (BIIB), which earned >$9bn of revenues last year.

Could it still be the case that, strong data and approval in 2026 aside, the market is overvaluing Revolution slightly? After all, if we assume no large Pharma is contemplating a takeover bid for Revolution, would analysts be more circumspect and wait to see daraxonrasib approved and perhaps even for a year or two of commercial sales data before assigning a >$25bn valuation?

Could Merck's interest in making an M&A deal in January and the possibility of its perhaps making a renewed bid for the company result in Wall Street overvaluing Revolution stock?

It's an interesting debate - on the one hand, Bristol-Myers Squibb paid <$5bn to acquire Mirati Therapeutics and adagrasib in 2023; on the other hand, Pfizer (PFE) bought out Seagen and its portfolio of recently approved antibody drug conjugate ("ADC") assets for ~$43bn in 2022.

Because the RAS pathway is such a valuable target in oncology, and because of Revolution's good data, I would certainly value its business much closer to Seagen than Mirati, but if no bidders emerge, Revolution will have to commercialize daraxonrasib itself, and it may take time to begin generating "blockbuster" revenues, plus the company made a deal with Royalty Pharma (RPRX), selling a chunk of future net sales of the drug in exchange for $2bn of funding.

All things considered, then, I'd be circumspect on the prospect of Revolution's share price continuing to rise and wonder if cheaper entry points will present themselves going forward?

If the company has truly cracked the RAS pathway conundrum, then it is hard to overstate the opportunity in front of the company, and a market cap of $50bn may not do its future earning potential justice.

Personally, however, I think I'd rather see the results of a few more pivotal studies and some early commercial sales data for daraxonrasib before investing in Revolution at the current share price and valuation.

data.title: Arista Networks: The Business Is Firing On All Cylinders But The Valuation Remains Reasonable
data.description:
Arista: An AI Infrastructure Vendor With Best-in-Class Profitability

It has been about a year since I last reviewed Arista (ANET) in an article on SA. It has been a rare tech stock that has shown meaningful appreciation over the past year. While the shares have risen by more than 100% over the past year, it doesn’t mean that they have escaped the tech apocalypse. (ANET is not a software company, so I can’t call what has happened to its valuation a SaaSpocalypse). The shares are up by a modest 11% so far this year. The shares are correlated to many other AI stocks and will respond notably to share price movement from other companies in the space. Most recently, over the last 5 trading days, and in the wake of three separate new buy recommendations from the analysts at Cowen, Rosenblatt, and Truist, the shares have risen by more than 12%.

An analyst at Hedgeye issued a sell recommendation; so far this analysis has not appeared to have resonated with many investors or with this author.

I am going to focus in this article on “what’s changed” for Arista over the last year rather than discuss any read-through from either macro issues or concerns about the AI infrastructure space. I, personally, think recession fears as expressed by some are off-base, and the fears of everyone’s favorite bugbear these days, stagflation, are dramatically overdone. There is nothing I can say about the war or its duration that hasn’t been said by many before. As I write this, there is a tenuous ceasefire, and this has led to some positive share price action for risk-on stocks.

This is an article about Arista and its investment opportunity. I believe that the shares, despite their recent spike, are a strong candidate for further recovery in an environment in which investors are less focused on geopolitical risks.

I recognize that the shares are unlikely to sustain durable positive alpha in what has been the current risk-off environment. And they will be susceptible to fears of a slowdown in the AI infrastructure supercycle. The opposite is also true.

Arista shares are volatile, and they respond notably to news about changes in the macro environment. Hopes of a speedy end to the Iran war have been and will likely continue to be significant in precipitating short-term share price moves. I, personally, think that trying to time an entry point in the shares is a bit like trying to hold mercury. I recommend buying the shares at their current price +/- a few percent. My buy recommendation for the shares is for the long term and not as a trade.

Arista: Its AI Infrastructure Journey

Arista reported the strongest percentage growth in revenues in several years when it published its Q4 earnings about 6 weeks ago. That was a level of growth that had been completely unanticipated just a couple of quarters ago. The company increased its forecast for revenue growth to 25% for this current year-again, also a level broadly unanticipated a few months ago.

The headline results were actually constrained because of a large non-recurring services renewal in the prior year quarter. Digging down, most of the increase in the company’s performance and its forecast relates to the rapidity of revenue growth coming from the AI data center segment, where the forecast for the current year was increased from $2.75 billion to $3.25 billion just in a single quarter. The forecast represents growth of more than 100% from the $1.5 billion the company reported for 2025. This performance in reported revenues was coupled with a strong increase in deferred revenues, which rose by 10% sequentially and by 95% year on year. While the company’s RPO disclosure is not entirely the same as that provided by other companies, it appears as though the company achieved a 10% sequential growth in bookings last quarter and a 50% growth in year-on-year bookings.

While initially the shares rose after the quarterly results and forecast were released, the shares subsequently fell victim to concerns about oil prices and other macro issues. Some analysts were concerned that the percentage revenue growth forecast wasn’t raised further.

One key factor in this kind of growth in revenues and backlog relates to the capex boom on the part of Arista’s Cloud Titan customers, particularly Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG). Meta and Microsoft have most recently accounted for over 40% of revenue last year, up from 35% the prior year. Although this is relatively significant customer concentration, what Arista calls “Cloud Titans” have been a substantial component of Arista’s revenues for all of the time it has been a public company. The company’s “other cloud” segment, which includes Oracle (ORCL) and CoreWeave (CRWV), is another 20% of revenues, and that proportion has been increasing. Google and AWS are customers, and according to some reports, Google may have recently made a substantial commitment to Arista for “front-end” AI networking. The company CEO strongly hinted about the contribution from these two customers during the latest conference call, but as these customers were less than 10% of revenue for the full year, their specific contribution hasn’t been reported.

Large customers such as these are very focused on avoiding any kind of technology lock-in. There are two technologies that have been used in building AI-focused data centers: InfiniBand and Ethernet. Arista has focused on Ethernet; that was a technology choice made years ago.

It has become apparent that for large clusters of the kind being used in AI infrastructure buildouts, Ethernet has a lower cost of ownership than its rival, InfiniBand. And for many workloads, the performance advantage in terms of latency between the two technologies has disappeared. I have linked to a couple of particular studies highlighting this.

As a result, Arista has been able to grow its market share noticeably. While Nvidia has a competitive Ethernet solution, it has not been able to keep up with some of the latest features, technologies, and partnerships that Arista offers. That said, in its last reported quarter, Nvidia Ethernet revenues did grow strongly, and most recently its market share in AI data center networking has been reported as 21%; that is for data center back-end switching. Looking at an apples-to-apples comparison, Arista most recently had a 21% share, Cisco had an 18% share, and Nvidia, while achieving the fastest percentage growth in the space, had a market share of about 11.6%.

Arista dominates what are called “Front-End” network switches, where its share has been 38%. I have linked here to an in-depth comparison between the two vendors. But I have quoted here from the study, which indicates that it isn’t a case of either/or, but both.

At this level you're not "ripping out" Arista for a NVIDIA environment. Traditional workloads still take place in most enterprise and even in Hyperscaler infrastructure. It is likely to have NVIDIA on the side, if you needed this scale, speed of their offering. If anything the two will be running side by side

From time to time, when Nvidia announces that one of Arista’s Titan customers is using the former company’s technology, Arista’s share price has come under some pressure. The fact is that while Nvidia has and will have business with the largest hyperscalers, that doesn’t imply that Arista is going to lose share in the Ethernet AI data center market. Although I have a smaller position in Arista shares than I believe to be optimal, I acquired the shares at different times when the story about Nvidia competition was rampant. If the controversy becomes newsworthy again and Arista shares decline, in my opinion that will be an excellent entry point.

NVIDIA claims ~1.6× better performance for large-scale AI jobs versus “traditional Ethernet,” thanks to its RoCE extensions and in-network acceleration. But Arista’s thesis is that with larger radix 800 G switches and smarter load-balancing, you can get close enough while keeping the commodity-Ethernet cost structure. Do you think the perf delta is big enough to justify Spectrum-X’s premium, or will hyperscalers push for open 800 G Ethernet to keep costs (and vendor leverage) down?

Finally, due to shortages, costs, supply chain, and other logistical factors it's super hard to get your hands on anything which is another reason to run both. "Marquee" Arista customers will continue to be marquee customers.

So far, the evidence suggests that Arista’s AI data center growth is more constrained by capacity than by demand. This is a quote by Jayshree Ullal, Arista's CEO, from the latest conference call transcript.

So, again, I am giving you the greatest visibility I can, fairly early in the year, on the reality of what we can ship, not what the demand might be. It might be a multiyear demand that ships over multiple years.

So let us hope it continues, but, of course, you must understand that we are also facing a law of large numbers. So 25% on a base of now $9,000,000,000 when we started last year at $8.25. It is a really, really early and good start.

Arista has had a long history of under-promising and over-delivering, and I think this is very likely to be the case when it comes to its current forecasts. I might also point out here that much of the capex from hyperscalers, Tier 2 cloud customers, and neo-cloud customers is taking place over time, and Arista will derive revenues from these orders it has already secured in arrears. It has been receiving orders for future delivery at an accelerated pace, but that translates into revenue over a period of years.

So long as the market share trends are positive, as they have been, the revenue for future years becomes more visible. The current published 1st Call consensus forecast for Arista revenue shows 27% growth this year, followed by 21% growth in 2027. My belief is that both of these numbers are too low by at least several hundred basis points, as the growth percentage for Arista’s offerings in AI data centers continues to secure more acceptance and as the space itself continues its hypergrowth.

Arista’s Other Growth Markets: Campus Switching

Essentially all of Arista’s guidance increase has related to the increasing forecast for sales of its AI Ethernet switches. About 30% of Arista’s business is now AI Ethernet switches, about 10% of its revenues are campus switches, and the balance might be thought of as the company’s legacy or core products. The guidance math suggests that this part of the business will not be growing. Management strongly pushed back against that thesis during the conference call. Basically, at this time, Arista’s growth is supply constrained-mainly memory components-for 2026. It can grow faster if it secures more supply. And its reported results may grow faster simply because it is likely to raise prices in order to best allocate demand.

One very successful initiative that is bearing fruit for Arista is that of campus switching. Campus switching revenue was about 8.5% of Arista’s total revenue last year and is projected to be 11% of revenue this year, with revenues growing by more than 60%. That said, Arista's market share in the campus switching space as calculated from the IDC data linked here is still just 3% - the upside is very large.

Arista’s share gains are based on technology - so called modern stacking, what is called AIOps, and cost performance advantages have been the primary drivers of market share gains. Probably the single most important capability driving market share is that of the company’s EOS, or its unified operating system. At this point, many users want an “all-Arista” stack and have chosen to migrate their campus requirements from legacy vendors-primarily HPE (HPE) and Cisco (CSCO).

Recently, the company announced what is called the XPO liquid-cooled optics module. This is a significant technology announcement with major improvements in performance and efficiency. It is based on universal standards. Some industry observers believe this announcement is a shot across the bow against Nvidia’s “co-packaged optics” offering. The recent upgrade recommendations I called out earlier in this article consider this to be a significant announcement with the potential for the new offering to create further growth inflection. There has been quite a bit of speculation that this new technology will be a material growth tailwind in the 2nd half of this year, with Microsoft potentially as an early significant customer.

The company has what it calls a “blue-box” offering. This enables hyperscalers to run their own software on Arista hardware. The Blue-Box offering is in its gestation period and is designed for hyperscalers and neo clouds. It is a key component of the company’s current success in its AI networking segment. It is Arista's answer to the many "white-box" alternatives in the market. It is most often deployed with high-capacity 800GB and 1.6TB systems. Nvidia doesn’t have an exact equivalent to the Blue Box offering, given that it is trying to sell its full hardware stack, but it does offer a similar capability by the use of alternative technology.

While most observers rightfully focus on the growth inflection that is being achieved by Arista’s AI networking and campus switching offerings, its core business is still growing. I think this is one of the unheralded components of the growth story and is a significant reason why I believe that current consensus estimates for both revenue growth and EPS are at levels that I expect will be notably surpassed.

Arista’s Competition

While I have focused on Nvidia as a competitor - and that is the principal focus of the current investment dialog - Cisco has always been Arista’s principal competitor, followed by Juniper, now part of HPE. The market is very divided between data center customers and customers for smaller deployments such as campus switching. Overall, the DC portion of the Ethernet switch market saw growth acceleration in the latest quarter to 63%. The non-DC market growth has only been a bit greater than 8%. The more dense 800 GB and above technologies are now starting to take off with growth of 92% sequentially and are now 18% of the total.

Arista is benefitting substantially from these trends, and I expect that this will continue to be the case for at least the next couple of years, and perhaps longer. At the moment, as mentioned, Arista has a 21%+ share of the market of the Data Center switch market. DC Switch revenues rose by a bit more than 30% year over year last quarter.

Cisco’s DC Ethernet switch revenues rose 17% year-on-year, and this reduced its DC switch market share to 17%. Nvidia’s Ethernet switch revenues jumped 168% year-on-year with a market share in the DC segment of 11.6%, although it has a 21% share when it comes to AI back-end switching. In terms of dollars, Arista’s DC switch revenues surpassed $2 billion last quarter, up by 31% year on year and a bit more than 5% sequentially.

The raw numbers for Nvidia may be a bit deceiving; heretofore most of Nvidia’s switching revenues were coming from Infiniband technology; for a variety of reasons, Infiniband is no longer being used in most new AI data centers. Nvidia had heretofore been the leader in sales of InfiniBand switching technology, and what is being seen in terms of the huge percentage increases in Nvidia’s Ethernet switching revenue is a function of migration as much as new customer capture.

Most industry analysts believe that Arista’s most important advantage is its operating system, EOS. Other advantages include CloudVision automation and an ultra-low latency solution for workloads such as those used by traders in financial institutions.

Cisco has a vast portfolio; Arista is far more focused on the more rap-segments of both the switching and routing market segments. These days, as most readers are well aware, power consumption has become a major concern in a data center. Arista’s architecture has always been focused on high port density, which leads to superior power efficiency. That alone would be sufficient for most DC users to choose Arista for their Ethernet switching requirements. The link above maintains that Arista's architecture and port density give it a 15%-30% power consumption advantage. Those kinds of claims are difficult to validate.

Nvidia is a formidable competitor. I have linked here to one of many 3rd-party analyses that have been published on this subject. Arista’s advantages relate to EOS, extremely high performance using 800 GB and now 1.6 TB technology, an open architecture, and better automation and observability

Nvidia offers a full-stack bundle, a key selling point for some users. I am not about to suggest that I have some unique answer as to who wins the competitive battle between Arista and Nvidia. My guess is that both companies will wind up with leadership market shares and will continue to gain share vis-à-vis Cisco and HPE/Juniper. There are all kinds of detailed nuances in evaluating capabilities, go-to-market strategies, total cost of ownership claims, front-end vs. back-end, and power efficiency. I am trying to evaluate Arista as an investment; it isn’t really necessary to resolve loads of competing claims, and, in any event, I am not the person who could accomplish that.

At this point, constructing a detailed, product-based model for Arista is more than a bit of a guess. While the company is forecasting growth of 117% in AI networking revenue to $3.25 billion this year, with stated upside potential depending on hyperscaler demand and capacity growth, there is no real way to try to evaluate growth in 2027. While there are plenty of “forecasts,” they are more like guesses than analyses. Given the size of Arista’s backlog and the acceleration of its bookings metric, I think that at the very least, the growth in dollars year on year in terms of growth will be reprised in 2027. i.e., about $1.75 billion in growth or $5 billion in Arista AI data center switching revenues. The advent of 1.6TB and even 3.2TB switching could be factors in Arista exceeding those kinds of projections. That kind of growth, coupled with campus switching growth and the newest optical networking capabilities, should be adequate to support a revenue CAGR of at least 25% in 2027/28.

Arista’s Business Model - One of the Most Profitable IT Hardware Vendors

Arista has enjoyed exceptional margins for many years now, and that attainment shows no signs of abatement despite the current shortage of memory. To review last quarter’s results, revenues were about 4% greater than the prior consensus. Overall revenue growth was almost 29% and was, as mentioned earlier, constrained because the year-earlier quarter included a one-time renewal of the company’s VeloCloud offering. Gross margins were 63.4%, slightly greater than the prior forecast but down by 80 bps from the prior year. This is a function of mix, with revenue gains strongest in the Cloud and AI Titan segment, which has lower gross margins.

The company’s OPEX is at exceptional levels, with R&D by far the largest category at 11% of revenue, unchanged in the last several quarters. Sales and marketing at 4% of revenues has continued to decline (sales and marketing dollars actually fell by a little bit last quarter), and G&A expense at a 1.1% ratio is as low as any other ratio for that category I have ever seen.

Overall, that brought non-GAAP operating margins to 47.5% last quarter and to 48.2% for the full year. The company is forecasting Q1 revenue growth of 25% and Q1 non-GAAP operating margins of a bit greater than 46%. I think one reason for what might seem to be a cautious revenue guide is that new products are making up a greater proportion of the mix. There may be specific acceptance requirements for fulfillment. Overall, growth in bookings is likely to continue to exceed the percentage growth in reported revenues over the course of the next year.

The cost of memory is having some impact on gross margins. That said, last year, Arista did not increase prices and absorbed the margin hit. That is not going to be the case this year. Those SKUs that are memory intensive have seen as much as 20% price increases. In addition, the company’s growth has enabled it to become a larger customer and get some volume discounts, and it has made significant forward purchase commitments to lock in some prices that have subsequently escalated. Despite the memory price spike, I think the guidance provided by Arista more than adequately compensates for what the company CEO describes as a "horrendous memory situation."

Last year, the company had a free cash flow margin of 47.2%, roughly consistent with its non-GAAP operating margins. As mentioned, because of the product mix of bookings, some of which have future acceptance criteria, deferred revenue growth was substantial. That is likely to be the case this year and could bring the free cash flow margin to a level slightly greater than operating margins. In the interests of prudence, I have used a projection of a free cash flow margin of 43% in analyzing the company’s valuation.

Arista does use a modest amount of stock-based compensation. SBC was less than 5% of revenues last year-not terribly surprising for a company that has relatively few employees for its scale. The company has offset SBC in recent years through share repurchase. It is projecting a similar setup this current year. I project that outstanding shares will continue to be at 1.276 billion.

Arista’s Valuation - Likely a Point of Some Controversy

Arista is not some under-the-radar company unknown or unloved. It doesn’t have anything like a compressed valuation. That said, it is far more profitable than its peers in the AI infrastructure space; it generates lots of cash, and its articulated guidance doesn’t truly encompass its growth potential. As mentioned, it has recently attracted some new recommendations from the analysts at Truist, Susquehanna, and Cowen and was upgraded on April 7th by the analysts at Rosenblatt. With these initiations and the upgrade, the average published price target has gone to $177. These latest upgrades are mainly focused on the company’s new optical product offering and its AI Ethernet data center share gains.

Based on my revenue projection for the next 12 months of $11.35 billion, the EV/S is about 13.85X. Given a projected free cash flow margin of 43% and a 3-year CAGR projection of 26%, the shares are selling at about an average valuation for their growth cohort. I consider that an attractive valuation given the sizeable backlog the company has built and is likely to continue building, in turn leading to better than normal visibility and the company’s continued market share gains in the Ethernet AI Data Center segment. While I doubt that the company’s margin can rise significantly from current levels, a 43% free cash flow margin and the current stock price equate to a free cash flow yield of about 3%. For a company achieving a notable growth inflection and with substantial visibility and a strengthening competitive position coupled with best-in-category profitability, I consider that kind of free cash flow margin as quite attractive.

Risks to the Investment Thesis

Arista is benefitting substantially from the AI infrastructure supercycle. It has a market-leading position in AI networking infrastructure. That said, it faces formidable competitors in Nvidia and Cisco, and it won’t win every competitive encounter. This in turn has led and will doubtless lead to periods of uncertainty and share price weakness as investors evaluate news about competition and orders from hyperscalers using competitive products.

While the company is forecasting that its AI infrastructure networking revenue will double this year, just how long that can continue is certainly not completely visible. I doubt strongly that revenues from that segment will double again in 2027. I do think that current published estimates are too low, but by how much is more of a guess than something of which I can be sure.

During the time that I was writing this article, the shares rose by 15% based on three new positive recommendations. And the shares are up further based on the recently announced war ceasefire. This is an article recommending the shares for the long term and not an article trying to suggest trading strategies for Arista shares. Despite the recent share price spike, the shares are still down by 8% over the last 6 months. The valuation has been significantly higher in the recent past given the strong recent growth in revenues and in free cash flow.

If the war ceasefire is something that is semi-permanent, it seems likely that lots of valuations will inflect positively. But readers may want to exercise prudence and patience and scale into an Arista position rather than buying a full position in one transaction.

The company is a consumer of memory, and memory prices are soaring. It is now having to raise prices on selected high-memory-usage SKUs to maintain gross margins. Overall, supply chain issues may constrain growth and margins, although I think it is notable that the company has a strong competence in dealing with these issues over the last several years and has made very substantial component purchase commitments that have mitigated to some degree the current component/memory shortages and pricing issues.

The company has a significant level of customer concentration-in particular, Meta and Microsoft most recently accounted for 42% of revenues, and there are two other 10%+ customers as yet unidentified. This has been typical for Arista for years, but it is obviously a risk.

Finally, it seems prudent to note geopolitical risks, and many others have already done so. When the company reports earnings next month, it may highlight those risks and issue cautious guidance. It has had a very cautious guidance philosophy that has often accounted for share price perturbation in the past.

Wrapping Up: The Case to Buy Arista Networks Shares Now

Arista is the leading supplier of Ethernet AI networking infrastructure, a category growing rapidly as part of the AI infrastructure supercycle. This segment of the company’s business is doubling this year and will be more than 30% of total revenues. The company has some unique technologies, particularly its EOS operating system, but also its newly announced optical capabilities. It has been successful in competing against Nvidia, both because many users do not want vendor lock-in and because of its strong and long-lived relationships with Cloud titans such as Microsoft and Meta.

The company's campus switching offerings are also achieving material absolute and market share growth.

The company’s reported revenue growth understates its actual go-to-market attainment, with calculated bookings growth accelerating, reaching more than 50% year-on-year last quarter. Operating margins and free cash flow margins are at a best-in-category level in the mid-40% range.

While the shares have recently spiked noticeably in the last few days, partially because of the war ceasefire but also because of several new buy recommendations from analysts at Truist, Rosenblatt, and Cowen, they still have what I believe to be an attractive valuation given the company’s revenue visibility and its history of conservative forecasting. I have a modest position in the shares currently and plan to add opportunistically; I am being prudent and patient and plan to build a position rather than buying a full position currently. I see significant positive alpha over the coming year.

data.title: Patient Capital Management Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
“The Worst Brings Out the Best in Us” - Dr. Edith Eger

Samantha McLemore

It’s been a busy quarter at Patient Capital.

First, we’re excited to announce we’ve hired a new analyst, Panpan Xiao. Panpan graduated from Columbia University’s Value Investing program last May with her MBA. Prior to that, she worked as an analyst at several places including Fidelity and Schroders. We’ve interviewed many candidates. Panpan’s flexible thinking, value orientation and history of delivering results made her a standout. Before business school, Panpan grew up in China and then worked in Europe covering international stocks. She will help accelerate our global research efforts. Join us in welcoming her.

The Opportunity Equity Strategy declined 5.96% in the first quarter, underperforming the benchmark S&P 500’s 4.33% decline. For the 1-year period, the portfolio gained 31.05% significantly outpacing the S&P 500’s 17.80% return. Since the start of the secular bull market in March 2009, the strategy delivered annualized returns of 17.88%, 153 basis points per year better than the S&P 500’s 16.35%. For the year-to-date period through this writing (Apr 8th), the market has recovered and portfolio is down 0.41% outperforming the S&P 500’s 0.61% loss.

Our performance in the quarter was better than recent past selloffs. We were down 1.38x as much as the index, which compares favorably to the 1.8x greater gains we’d earned in the prior three quarters (40.89% vs. 23.13%).

In the first quarter of 2025, the third quarter of 2023 and the first quarter of 2022 (the initial quarter in that market decline), the S&P declined a comparable 3.27%-4.60% and we were down 1.5x to 2.2x as much. While we think short-term downside matters much less than long-term returns, many investors care greatly about these metrics.

That said, we believe the secular bull market that began in March 2009 is intact. If we’re correct, the optimal strategy is to buy on weakness. Leadership is likely to remain the same (Mag 7 and technology).

If we’re wrong, the biggest risk is a significant bear market like we saw at the end of the Tech Bubble or the Nifty Fifty period. In those situations, a value oriented portfolio cushioned you against the worst losses.

We don’t think a significant bear market is imminent. The usual prerequisites don’t exist. The Fed is unlikely to raise rates and recession remains improbable. The selloff took the S&P 500’s P/E back to levels (19.1x) we haven’t seen since 2023 and relieved pockets of excess.

Given recent moves, we see ample attractive opportunities, though they are dispersed across the market. The top 10 companies in the S&P 500 now trade at a smaller premium to their historical average than the remaining 490. Value outperformed growth for the second consecutive quarter, bringing relative valuations back to their long-term average. Small cap growth now trades at a discount to its 20-year average valuation (P/E), the best of any style box.

During the quarter, the S&P declined 9.8% from its January peak to its March 30th low. Since the secular bull market began, the S&P has declined 5%, 31 previous times, a rate of nearly twice per year. It’s lost 10% ten times, slightly more than once every other year. Volatility occurs regularly and usually represents a buying opportunity.

Beneath the surface, moves were extreme. Some private credit names were crushed. Software declined 35% from its peak late last year. Bitcoin’s peak-to-trough loss hit 52%, while Ark Innovation ETF’s loss hit 32%. Some former darlings, like Trade Desk ((TTD) $22.69) and Duolingo ((DUOL) $98.57), are down roughly 80% from their 52-week highs.

Market prices gyrate more dramatically than intrinsic business values, which means violent swings make mispricings more likely. We’ve dedicated significant time and research this quarter to software, payments companies (another area hit hard) and private credit.

Software’s taken a beating due to concerns about AI disruption, but we aren’t finding clear widespread opportunities. Before fears arose, beloved software names carried lofty valuations. Through the peak in October, software had gained 19.9% per year1, 540bps better than the S&P 500, from the time Marc Andreesen famously stated that “software is eating the world” in Aug 2011.

At that time, the enterprise value-to-operating income of the group was ~11x. By October 2025, the multiple nearly tripled to ~28x, making outperformance more challenging. The all-time high of ~45x occurred in the 2020-2021 pandemic period. (These companies’ prodigious issuance of stock compensation makes this metric better than others).

As we write this, valuations have fallen back to about 18x, the low end of the past decade’s range. All else equal, a potentially compelling level. But all is not equal. AI represents a legitimate competitive threat. For most software companies, market embedded expectations still imply companies can sustain growth and returns for more than a decade.

This is certainly possible, but it’s difficult to get conviction when most embedded value creation occurs in the distant future and disruption risks grow.

We found one company we thought was a bargain: Adobe ((ADBE) $243.08). Adobe’s stock price peaked in 2021 at $700 per share. It’s faced competitive threats, namely in Figma ((FIG) $21.14) and Canva, for the better part of the last half decade. As a result, the price and valuation have compressed more than other names.

The stock has a 10%+ free cash flow yield – a level historically associated with excess returns. Additionally, the company responded to threats with strategically sound adjustments including a new freemium tier for consumers and small businesses. We think the Enterprise business is insulated by the IP risk protection it offers.

Adobe is still growing topline and profits in the high single digits. We estimate the price discounts only four more years of growth. If it can grow 7.5% for the next 5 years then transitions to inflationary level growth, we estimate the stock is worth close to $400 per share (+65%).

The company has been aggressively allocating free cash flow to share repurchases. Shares fell 5.1% in fiscal 2025, and 3.7% in the last quarter. We think the combination of continued growth and prodigious cash generation, which is returned to shareholders, is powerful.

What would make us wrong? Competitive threats that erode business fundamentals, which we’ve yet to see. The company also has room to cut costs aggressively – a benefit in any scenario.

We like companies with high free cash flow yields that aggressively buy back stock and shrink shares outstanding. We own several: Adobe, Crocs ((CROX) $83.02), General Motors ((GM) $74.50), Expedia ((EXPE) $230.89) and JD.com ((JD) $29.57). The depressed valuations protect against potential market-driven valuation headwinds. Repurchases create demand for the stock and improve intrinsic values (if done below them, which is a requirement for us).

Private credit also troubled investors in the quarter. Fraud at Tricolor and First Brands, along with software exposure fears, led to massive investor redemptions in private credit portfolios. Private credit rapidly expanded over the past decade, which led to loosening credit standards. Higher interest rates also challenged returns.

Overall, systemic risk appears minimal. Corporate debt relative to GDP is falling - a stark contrast to the years preceding the tech bubble and global financial crisis. Bank balance sheets remain strong, especially at the largest banks. We monitor closely but believe risks will remain contained.

So far, we haven’t made any investments in the space. BDCs currently trade at 0.75x their Net Asset Values, but we don’t yet think returns compensate for the risk. The Alternative Asset Managers are down 25% to start the year and look more compelling. They have more diversified and durable revenue streams, high margin profiles, and significantly less software exposure.

We used pressure in the crypto space to add to positions in Bitcoin and Coinbase. We have long-term conviction in both. We see bitcoin as early-stage digital gold. This does not imply any level of stability; it implies a special psychological belief state. Bitcoin remains volatile, though volatility has been declining. If the value of bitcoin outstanding rises to gold’s level over coming decades, the appreciation potential is enormous.

We view Coinbase as a platform for crypto and blockchain based services. We’ve seen significant progress on fundamental business drivers, with stablecoins, tokenization and Base (transaction times and costs) progressing nicely.

At the same time, we are prioritizing companies we think can do well even in the most adverse scenarios, like Royalty Pharma ((RPRX) $47.97), our largest position, and UnitedHealth ((UNH) $270.59), which we’ve added to. We think both can deliver double-digit returns in most scenarios.

To summarize, market volatility creates opportunities. We’ve used recent moves to position the portfolio for strong long-term risk-adjusted returns. We think the bull market is intact, and buying weakness will prove profitable. We can always be wrong and attempt to position the portfolio to outperform even in an adverse scenario.

1As measured by the S&P 500 Application Software Index

FOR INSTITUTIONAL INVESTORS ONLY

Note: Individual stock prices as of 3/31/26

The views expressed in this commentary reflect those of Patient Capital Management portfolio managers as of the date of the commentary. Any views are subject to change at any time based on market or other conditions, and Patient Capital Management disclaims any responsibility to update such views. These views are not intended to be a forecast of future events, a guarantee of future results or investment advice. Because investment decisions are based on numerous factors, these views may not be relied upon as an indication of trading intent on behalf of any portfolio. Any data cited herein is from sources believed to be reliable, but is not guaranteed as to accuracy or completeness. The information presented should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any security and should not be relied upon as investment advice. It should not be assumed that any purchase or sale decisions will be profitable or will equal the performance of any security mentioned. References to specific securities are for illustrative purposes only. Portfolio composition is shown as of a point in time and is subject to change without notice.

All historical financial information is unaudited and shall not be construed as a representation or warranty by us. data.title: Trump's Hormuz Siege: Starving Tehran To Save The West
data.description:
The situation is becoming increasingly confusing

As I expected, the discussions over the weekend didn’t bring the much-awaited peace to the Middle East. Even though the ceasefire between US-Iran is still valid, the situation is rapidly evolving into a new phase where the US is growing its military presence around the Strait of Hormuz: a ground invasion seems no longer so unlikely. On the other hand, Iran doesn’t want to take a step back and threatens to retaliate against any attack.

This war was supposed to last at best 6 weeks. We have just crossed that deadline, and an immediate end is now excluded. Of course, the longer the war goes on, the more it will hurt the global economy.

Last week, the ceasefire talks flooded the market with optimism, and the main stock market indexes had almost recovered the lost ground.

However, the last weekend acted as a reality check; in fact, both US and Iranian negotiators have left the Islamabad meeting with more questions than answers. While I am writing, financial markets are acting accordingly.

US stock market futures are down ~ 0.50%, WTI Crude Oil is surging by 8.50%, and it is heading toward $120 per barrel, a resistance level that has been felt twice since the beginning of the war.

As you can notice from this chart, even though WTI crude oil has suffered multiple crashes following positive news from the war, its trend has always remained bullish. It presents higher lows, and for the reasons I am going to explain, I believe it now has the potential to break above the $120 resistance. As a result, the stock market might soon realize that this war has a much greater impact than expected. Therefore, I am ready for a new wave of pessimism.

Trump's New Strategy: A smart move or too reckless?

Since the US and Iran left Pakistan with no agreement, President Trump decided to put pressure on Iran with a new strategy: block Iranian ports. Blocking the Iranian ports is equal to destroying the entire Iranian economy. Here is why.

Oil and gas exports are the only reason why Iran presents a positive account balance. If you block Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, about 90% of the Iranian oil can’t be sold (83% of all Iranian exports). Iran would immediately face extremely negative economic consequences, as if the current state of its economy were not weak enough. With no money, Iran can’t sustain the war and would be forced to accept the peace terms proposed by the US.

So, at first glance, this seems a smart move able to hurt Iran in a significant manner. However, this war is teaching us that expectations don’t always match the reality. Actually, they don’t most of the time. The Strait of Hormuz should have already been opened a couple of weeks ago, and the Iranians shouldn’t have been capable of shutting down US cutting-edge aircraft. Therefore, I have many doubts that this time everything will evolve in the best way possible.

I believe this operation has the potential to escalate things to the next level, which is oil at $150 per barrel and a stock market suffering a new wave of pessimism, even worse than that experienced in March. The reasons behind my view are mainly four:

  • First of all, there is no clear indication how this blockade will work. If you fire missiles against all tankers that come and go from Iranian ports, chances are that in a couple of days the entire world would suffer an environmental disaster. So, since you can’t shut down these tankers, the only option left is to force them to change route. In the worst-case scenario, it might be necessary to take physical control of these tankers. However, I wonder how China and Spain (two countries allowed by Iran to cross the Strait) would react when they know that their tankers have been attacked. Maybe, tariffs can soon get the front page of newspapers once again.
  • The choice to block the Iranian ports shows a clear contradiction with the recent choice to lift sanctions on Iranian oil in order to sustain the energy supply chain. In my opinion, the US is realizing that opening the Strait is much harder than expected; that’s why it is now shifting the strategy to damage Iran from an economic perspective. Regardless of the outcome, the markets don’t appreciate periods of confusion where the future looks cloudy. Finally, closing the Strait to Iranian ports creates even more panic across the few tankers that were previously allowed to cross it. So, in this context of shrinking supply, oil prices have significant upside pressure, and this is not what the global economy wants.
  • The US strategy to weaken the Iranian economy makes sense, but only if it doesn’t crash the global economy first: it is a race against time. The Iranians must give up pretty soon; otherwise, the high oil prices will likely bring us back to the 1970s. I am not sure the Iranians will; in fact, they are historically a very resilient population that has been dealing with economic sanctions for 50 years. Compared to us Westerners, they are not afraid of poverty or of a stock market crash.
  • Nobody questions the US superiority from a military point of view, but downplaying Iran’s ability to retaliate against US attacks on the ports could be a serious mistake. The US has been claiming victory since day 1, and if Iran had been annihilated as affirmed, the Strait would be open. In addition, if Iran had really no options left, it would have accepted all US conditions during the last meeting in Pakistan.

All these reasons make me believe we are not done yet with this war, which is why I hold my cautionary approach. I am buying shares of some resilient companies like Domino’s, Visa, and S&P Global, but I still maintain a significant amount of cash. I am avoiding risk-on assets (tech stocks, discretionary goods stocks) as I believe we are going to face turbulent times ahead.

Conclusion

The length of this war is getting out of control and threatens the global economy through sky-high energy prices; I believe the stock market will soon realize it. The conflict in the Middle East is ramified across multiple countries, and a serious peace talk should include Israel as well; among the reasons why Iran rejected the peace was the Israeli unwillingness to stop bombing Lebanon.

Furthermore, the US's constant change of target is not helping to bring down the uncertainty. First, it was the regime change, then the destruction of the nuclear and missile program, and now the opening of a Strait that was already opened before the war. Meanwhile, oil prices are heading higher and are firmly above $100 per barrel. Time is ticking, and the US needs to resolve a situation that seems more complex than 45 days ago.

Last Friday, the energy component of the headline CPI showed concerning results: the gasoline index increased 21.20%, the largest monthly increase since the series was first published in 1967.
